markdown.markdown:
```{r} #| label: setup #| include: false options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE) knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = TRUE, fig.asp = 0.5625, out.width = "100%", fig.retina = 2, dpi = 300 ) set.seed(20251108) library("tidyverse") library("here") library("knitr") theme_psllt <- function(...) { list( theme_bw(base_family = "Palatino", ...), theme( plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40"), panel.grid.major = element_line(color = 'grey90', linewidth = 0.15), panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = 'grey90', linewidth = 0.15) ) ) } theme_set(theme_grey(base_size = 30)) ``` ##
**[Who are we?]{.emph}**
- We are RU Bilingual - Graduate students in the Spanish & Portuguese department at Rutgers - We study bilingualism and second language acquisition ##
**[What do we do?]{.emph}**
- We want our day-to-day research to be useful to a broader public - We want to create a safe place to discuss multilingualism in our communities ##
**[Our objectives:]{.emph}**
- Support and defend bilingualism at home, in school, and in our community - Allow for academic research to have more practical applications # What we'll be talking about today {.transition} ::: incremental - What is bilingualism? - Linguistic Attitudes & Language Ideologies - What myths exist about bilingualism? - What advantages are there to bilingualism? - How can we contribute to maintaining bilingualism? ::: # What is Bilingualism? {.transition} Do you consider yourself bilingual? ::: notes Discuss in pairs, then share. ::: ## Bilingualism - The ability to understand and/or communicate in more than one language - Is there a minimum requirement to be considered bilingual? ::: fragment Four components:
read / write / speak / listen
::: ## Bilingualism versus Biliteracy - [Biliteracy]{.emph}: ability to read and write in two languages - A [bilingual person]{.emph} is not necessarily biliterate - Some bilinguals [cannot read/write]{.emph} in their two languages - [Less than half]{.emph} of the world's languages today are written [@sil_orthography_development] - About [12%] of the human population is illiterate today [@unesco2025factsheet] ::: notes Mention that for the vast majority of history, humans did not write. Today, the vast majority of languages remain solely oral. ::: ## Bilingualism in the world ::: incremental - How many languages are there in the world? - More than 6,900! - How many countries are there? - 206 ::: ::: fragment More than [33 languages per country]{.emph} The truth: [More than half]{.emph} of children around the world grow up in a [bilingual environment]{.emph} ::: ::: notes Many languages are moribund, and we have seen a major extinction event of languages due to colonialism. ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="80%"} ![](assets/bilingualism_usa.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="20%"} **[22%]{.emph}** of people in the U.S. speak a language other than English ::: ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="70%"} ![](assets/bilingualism_nj.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} **[34%]{.emph}** of people in New Jersey speak a language other than English ::: ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="80%"} ![](assets/somerset.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="20%"} **[37%]{.emph}** of people in Somerset County speak a language other than English ::: ::: ::: notes factoid source: https://www.census.gov/acs/www/about/why-we-ask-each-question/language/ map sources for usa & nj: https://lang-map.mla.org/?_ga=2.189842600.266919070.1776158169-1032903345.1776158169&l=Total_Lower_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Upper_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Graduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Institutional_Enrollments!%2CPredominantPrimaryLesson%5B20%5D!%2Cpredominant_language_spoken_excl_english%5B20%5D!%2Cpredominant_language_spoken_excl_spanish_eng%5B20%5D~%2Call_languages_other_engl_pct%2Cother_unspecified_languages_pct!%2Cafrican_languages_pct!%2Carabic_pct!%2Carmenian_pct!%2Cchinese_pct!%2Cfrench_pct!%2Cfrench_creole_pct!%2Cgerman_pct!%2Cgreek_pct!%2Cgujarathi_pct!%2Chebrew_pct!%2Chindi_pct!%2Chmong_pct!%2Chungarian_pct!%2Citalian_pct!%2Cjapanese_pct!%2Ckorean_pct!%2Claotian_pct!%2Cmonkmer_pct!%2Cnavajo_pct!%2Cpersian_pct!%2Cpolish_pct!%2Cportugese_pct!%2Crussian_pct!%2Cscandanavian_pct!%2Cserbian_croatian_pct!%2Cspanish_pct!%2Ctagalog_pct!%2Cthai_pct!%2Curdu_pct!%2Cvietnamese_pct!%2Cyiddish_pct!%2Cother_asian_pct!%2Cother_indic_pct!%2Cother_indoeuropean_pct!%2Cother_pacificisland_pct!%2Cother_slavic_pct!%2Cother_naindigenous_pct!%2Cother_westgermanic_pct!%2Call_languages_other_engl_cnt!%2Cother_unspecified_languages_cnt!%2Cafrican_languages_cnt!%2Carabic_cnt!%2Carmenian_cnt!%2Cchinese_cnt!%2Cfrench_cnt!%2Cfrench_creole_cnt!%2Cgerman_cnt!%2Cgreek_cnt!%2Cgujarathi_cnt!%2Chebrew_cnt!%2Chindi_cnt!%2Chmong_cnt!%2Chungarian_cnt!%2Citalian_cnt!%2Cjapanese_cnt!%2Ckorean_cnt!%2Claotian_cnt!%2Cmonkmer_cnt!%2Cnavajo_cnt!%2Cpersian_cnt!%2Cpolish_cnt!%2Cportugese_cnt!%2Crussian_cnt!%2Cscandanavian_cnt!%2Cserbian_croatian_cnt!%2Cspanish_cnt!%2Ctagalog_cnt!%2Cthai_cnt!%2Curdu_cnt!%2Cvietnamese_cnt!%2Cyiddish_cnt!%2Cother_asian_cnt!%2Cother_indic_cnt!%2Cother_indoeuropean_cnt!%2Cother_pacificisland_cnt!%2Cother_slavic_cnt!%2Cother_naindigenous_cnt!%2Cother_westgermanic_cnt!&bl=mapnik&t=qgis_mapserv.fcgi&c=-10710812%2C4732693&s=20000000 map source for somerset county: https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/cbd39e908efc459abbc2aa51916aed09/page/Language-Spoken-At-Home ::: # Linguistic Attitudes & Language Ideologies {.transition} ## Despite the [large]{.emph} number of bilinguals in the world, and especially in the United States, people still have [very different reactions]{.emph} to bilingualism. ## Let's reflect... 1. Is one language "better" than another? Why? 2. Which language sounds more romantic? Where does that idea come from? 3. Which variety of Spanish is "better"? Mexican? Colombian? Another? 4. How do young people speak today? 5. Do you think your parents thought the same way at your age? 6. What assumptions do we make about how wealthy people speak? 7. What about people with less money? ::: notes discuss with a partner ::: ## {.smaller} [Language attitudes]{.emph} are reactions toward languages, their use in society, and their speakers. These attitudes can influence how we speak. "Language" here variably refers to... - A language (e.g., Spanish) - A variety (e.g., Mexican Spanish) - A style (e.g., youth speech) - A sociolect (e.g., middle-class speech) Attitudes can be: - Positive - Negative - Neutral ## ::: incremental Three common ways of evaluating language - Correctness → Correct vs. Incorrect - Appropriateness → Better vs. Worse - Aesthetics → Pleasant vs. Ugly ::: ::: fragment But... ::: ::: fragment [Who]{.emph} decides what is correct, better, or pleasant? ::: ## {.smaller} ::: fragment - Linguistic Attitudes - Evaluations or reactions toward languages - Expressed as opinions, beliefs, or prejudices - Individual level ::: ::: fragment - Language Ideologies - Socially constructed ideas about how language should function - Linked to political, social, and economic interests - Shared across groups or societies ::: ::: fragment - Some research - @callesano2019latinx: perceptions of Spanish varieties - @sima2017actitudes: pride and identity in Yucatec Maya - @purnell1999perceptual: linguistic profiling ::: ##

"Contemporary raciolinguistic ideologies must be situated within colonial histories that shaped the co-naturalization of language and race as part of the project of modernity" [@rosa2017unsettling] ## What do [colonial processes]{.emph} do? ::: fragment They [systematize]{.emph} and [classify]{.emph} language: ::: ::: fragment - Appropriate vs. inappropriate - Intelligent vs. unintelligent - e.g.: Grammar of the Castilian Language [@nebrija1492gramatica] ::: ## Raciolinguistic ideologies in early colonization - Applied to Indigenous populations - Language used to justify hierarchies and difference - Today: the “white listening subject” ## Raciolinguistic ideologies in early colonization - Certain ways of speaking are evaluated through white, normative expectations - Raciolinguistics today: - Stigmatization of Latinx English and Spanish in the U.S. - Stigmatization of dialects that have been previously racialized ## Language attitudes and ideologies also shape the concept of bilingualism - [Monoglossic ideologies]{.emph} - "One nation, one language" - shapes educational policies - The reification of the "native speaker" ## Who counts as a "native speaker"? Research shows this figure is often imagined as: - White - Middle or upper class - From specific regions ::: fragment
These [assumptions]{.emph} lead to [misdiagnosis]{.emph} and [misplacement]{.emph} of students in educational systems.
::: ## We are often told that being bilingual is an advantage. But... an advantage for whom? Zavala and Tupas argue that bilingualism is not a neutral category — it is political. Peru “Bilingual” is often used for Quechua-Spanish students, sometimes with deficit connotations. Philippines English-Filipino bilingualism erased the presence of more than 100 local languages. So we should ask: What counts as bilingual in your context? Only English and Spanish? What about Indigenous languages? Bilingualism is not fixed. It is a diverse and situated experience. # What myths exist about bilingualism? {.transition} ## True or false? 1) Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. 2) You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. 3) Your ability to speak your languages do not change over your lifetime. ::: notes Discuss with a partner ::: ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. FALSE. No hay separación de idiomas, los lenguajes están interconectados como si fueran parte de una tela de araña, si un hilo se mueve, hace vibrar toda la tela de araña. ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. - A bilingual's two languages are always "on" [@paradis1993linguistic] - This is important when we want to speak and read in one language ![](assets/whackamole.jpg) ::: notes Nuestros idiomas son como los topitos de Whack a mole, están siempre intentado salir y nosotros tenemos que aplastarlos para que nos dejen hablar el idioma que queremos en el momento que queremos. ::: ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. - Sometimes we use the wrong word: - What I want to say: "Pass me the [fork]{.emph}." - What I actually say by accident: "Pass me the [knife]{.emph}." ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. - A multilingual speaker's languages are always competing for activation - Activation can depend on... - semantics - phonetics - syntax ::: notes Give an example of each with Spanish & English Add images for this: Coche – Car – Casa (Coche activa Car y Car es competidor fonológico de Casa) ::: # You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. FALSE. Bilingualism comes in all shapes and sizes. People who study dead languages like Latin or Greek might only be able to [read]{.emph}. Many languages do not have written forms. Many people who grow up in the US are exposed to a language other than English at home, and there are many outcomes: - can only comprehend spoken language - can speak and comprehend, but cannot read or write - can read, write, speak, and comprehend spoken language ## Your ability to speak your languages do not change over your lifetime. Language attrition. Switch dominance. # What do we think about bilingualism? {.transition} ## True or false? Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. FALSE - Monolingual children are evaluated in phases - Bilingual children too! - Problem: the same scale exam is normally used to evaluate these two different populations. THE TRUTH: add some truths & clean up the FALSE statements ## True or false? Bilingual children sometimes mix their languages. Are they confused? Is it too hard to speak two languages? FALSE - Bilingual children know what language to speak and with who ADD CITATION AND AGE - Mixing languages isn't a sign of confusion or laziness THE TRUTH: - Bilinguals who mix languages actually know with whom to mix and when it's (not) appropriate. ADD CITATION - There are [rules]{.emph} to mixing languages -- it's not random! - Mixing both at the same time is a sign of advanced knowledge of the two languages! ADD CITATION - We call this mixing [codeswitching]{.emph} ::: notes La comprensión y la producción de un bilingüe es diferente de la producción y la comprensión de un monolingüe porque los idiomas están siempre encendidos. ::: ## Mixing languages Rate these sentences on how natural they seem to you: - Can you peinarme? - I am estrenando this t-shirt. - El plato está en el sink. ADD UNNATURAL CODESWITCHES ## Codeswitching and Spanglish "Es una combinación de fenómenos de contacto distintos. Estos fenómenos incluyen préstamos, calcos, extensiones semánticas, préstamos temporales, mezclas, entre otros" [@casielles2017spanglish] - troca ~ truck - llamar pa'trás ~ to call back "Spanglish is what we speak, who we Latinos are, and how we act and how we perceive the world" [@morales2007living] ## Codeswitching - Estás ready? - Vamos a janguear. - Vamos para el mall. "Si quieres empezar otra pelea, I'm not in the mood. Anyway, vine a otra cosa" [@prida1991beautiful] Do you have other examples? ## Spanglish in the Wild add images here of spanglish # Are There Advantages to Being Bilingual? {.transition} Add Stroop Test here. Multilingualism & brain health (check study from Nuria's class) Multilingualism & Alzheimer's (check study from Nuria's class) # How Can we Contribute to Maintaining Bilingualism? {.transition} ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? ADD IMAGES OF FAMILY ACTIVITIES AND STUFF ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? The language(s) should be spoken whenever possible - talk about daily activities - tell stories and histories - ask questions - [motivate everyone to participate]{.emph} USE YOUR LANGAUGE! ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? Create a [network]{.emph} of speakers - Look for community activities and people outside your home - Don't be afraid of using different languages at home - Interact with others in your target language ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Tell or read stories in the target language - Listening is important too! - Promote reading and writing skills by teaching or actively modeling these skills ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Create safe place for people to communicate in their languages Zimmerli picture # DISORDERED SPEECH https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DJVz0rNJ0BA&list=PLnfCMjNTvILC8q1cknIk_fvzOfOV_oZhB&index=191 Use this to talk about bilingual language development and disorders Check out Patrick's work for overdiagnosis of disordered speech in bilingual children. ## Thanks! {.final visibility="uncounted"} ::: {.absolute top="20" left="0"}

FALSE. Bilingualism comes in all shapes and sizes. People who study dead languages like Latin or Greek might only be able to [read]{.emph}. Many languages do not have written forms. Many people who grow up in the US are exposed to a language other than English at home, and there are many outcomes: - can only comprehend spoken language - can speak and comprehend, but cannot read or write - can read, write, speak, and comprehend spoken language ## Your ability to speak your languages do not change over your lifetime. Language attrition. Switch dominance. # What do we think about bilingualism? {.transition} ## True or false? Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. FALSE - Monolingual children are evaluated in phases - Bilingual children too! - Problem: the same scale exam is normally used to evaluate these two different populations. THE TRUTH: add some truths & clean up the FALSE statements ## True or false? Bilingual children sometimes mix their languages. Are they confused? Is it too hard to speak two languages? FALSE - Bilingual children know what language to speak and with who ADD CITATION AND AGE - Mixing languages isn't a sign of confusion or laziness THE TRUTH: - Bilinguals who mix languages actually know with whom to mix and when it's (not) appropriate. ADD CITATION - There are [rules]{.emph} to mixing languages -- it's not random! - Mixing both at the same time is a sign of advanced knowledge of the two languages! ADD CITATION - We call this mixing [codeswitching]{.emph} ::: notes La comprensión y la producción de un bilingüe es diferente de la producción y la comprensión de un monolingüe porque los idiomas están siempre encendidos. ::: ## Mixing languages Rate these sentences on how natural they seem to you: - Can you peinarme? - I am estrenando this t-shirt. - El plato está en el sink. ADD UNNATURAL CODESWITCHES ## Codeswitching and Spanglish "Es una combinación de fenómenos de contacto distintos. Estos fenómenos incluyen préstamos, calcos, extensiones semánticas, préstamos temporales, mezclas, entre otros" [@casielles2017spanglish] - troca ~ truck - llamar pa'trás ~ to call back "Spanglish is what we speak, who we Latinos are, and how we act and how we perceive the world" [@morales2007living] ## Codeswitching - Estás ready? - Vamos a janguear. - Vamos para el mall. "Si quieres empezar otra pelea, I'm not in the mood. Anyway, vine a otra cosa" [@prida1991beautiful] Do you have other examples? ## Spanglish in the Wild add images here of spanglish # Are There Advantages to Being Bilingual? {.transition} Add Stroop Test here. Multilingualism & brain health (check study from Nuria's class) Multilingualism & Alzheimer's (check study from Nuria's class) # How Can we Contribute to Maintaining Bilingualism? {.transition} ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? ADD IMAGES OF FAMILY ACTIVITIES AND STUFF ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? The language(s) should be spoken whenever possible - talk about daily activities - tell stories and histories - ask questions - [motivate everyone to participate]{.emph} USE YOUR LANGAUGE! ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? Create a [network]{.emph} of speakers - Look for community activities and people outside your home - Don't be afraid of using different languages at home - Interact with others in your target language ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Tell or read stories in the target language - Listening is important too! - Promote reading and writing skills by teaching or actively modeling these skills ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Create safe place for people to communicate in their languages Zimmerli picture # DISORDERED SPEECH https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DJVz0rNJ0BA&list=PLnfCMjNTvILC8q1cknIk_fvzOfOV_oZhB&index=191 Use this to talk about bilingual language development and disorders Check out Patrick's work for overdiagnosis of disordered speech in bilingual children. ## Thanks! {.final visibility="uncounted"} ::: {.absolute top="20" left="0"}
